toolchain.note: Upstream publishes ONNX directly; no paddle2onnx step. Shapes fixed with onnxslim 0.1.59.

dictionary.layout: line 0 = 'blank' placeholder, then characters, then a trailing space
dictionary.warning: medium and small share this charset; tiny is a separate 6906-class charset and must use its own file

task_metric.name: greedy CTC decode matches PaddleOCR output on the same crop
task_metric.measured_onnx: PASS at 48x960; at 48x320 a 19.8:1 crop loses characters to the aspect squeeze (expected, not a defect)

measured.note: Both models compiled to .hbm on OE 3.7.0 (HBRT 4.7.5). All int8 PTQ. Not yet board-run, so no latency or end-to-end accuracy.

det.gelu_placement: all 28 GELU nodes ON=BPU (no CPU fallback)

rec.calibration: 64 real line crops, cut from the 24 det pages by the det ONNX

measured.assessment: Both output cosines (0.98 det, 0.97 rec) sit below the 0.99 layer-A gate. This is int8 on transformer-ish OCR heads and is expected; DB detection thresholds its probability map and CTC recognition is argmax-per-step, so both tolerate some fidelity loss. Whether that costs real recall / character accuracy is a BOARD question -- compare against an int16 build only if it does. Do not fail these on cosine alone (see CONVERSION.md). BOARD RESULT: det layer B bit-identical; rec layer B task-equivalent (2e-4 logit drift, identical decode). Layer C confirms real int8 character loss on a hard crop -- an int16 rec build is the next experiment if accuracy matters.

open_questions: RESOLVED for detection: all 28 GELU (Erf/HzGelu) nodes show ON=BPU in node_info, i.e. no CPU fallback. ON=BPU is trustworthy (unlike ON=--). Latency still unmeasured until board.
Recognition (SVTR stack) not yet compiled. If int8 output cosine is poor, read per-layer cosine only to localise, then consider int16 -- see CONVERSION.md.
Detection output cosine 0.98 (int8): compare against an int16 det build if box recall degrades on the board.

layer_C_hbm_vs_onnx.interpretation: Layer B is bit-identical, so this is pure int8 quantisation loss, not a port defect. DB detection thresholds its probability map; whether 0.98 costs box recall needs a boxed comparison, not a cosine.

layer_B_hbm_vs_bc.interpretation: board and host decode to identical text, but the logits differ by 2e-4. This model is mixed int8/int16/int32 with float CPU ops; board and host float implementations differ in the last places. Bit-identity holds for pure fixed-point graphs (las2, det), not necessarily for mixed ones. The right layer-B check here is task equivalence.

layer_C_hbm_vs_onnx.interpretation: Real int8 loss: board int8 decode drops several characters the float ONNX keeps. On this deliberately hard 48x320 crop (a 19.8:1 line squeezed to 6.7:1) int8 is visibly worse. This is the concrete answer to whether int8 rec is good enough: on hard inputs, not without an int16 comparison.

int16_recognition.why: int8 recognition looked poor; separating the preprocessing error from the quantisation error was the key.
int16_recognition.critical_finding: The dominant error was preprocessing, not quantisation. A plain cv2.resize to (320,48) STRETCHES short lines; PaddleOCR/BCDL use aspect-preserving resize + right-pad. On float ONNX (no quantisation) the two preprocessings disagree on 16/20 short crops, pad being right. Both calib.py and evaluation now use pad.
int16_recognition.method: 20 aspect-fitting real line crops (<=6.7:1), correct pad preprocessing on calibration AND evaluation, board decode vs float ONNX decode.
int16_recognition.calibration_cosine_improvement: Fixing the calibration preprocessing raised the int8 rec output cosine from 0.9747 (stretch) to 0.9819 (pad) on its own, before any bit-width change.

layer_B.int8: cosine 1.0, 2e-4 drift (mixed precision, float CPU ops)

int16_recognition.recommendation: Use int16 (config_rec_int16.yaml) when accuracy matters -- it more than halves CER. int8 is fine for easy well-cropped Latin text; most of its errors are single-glyph. Latency of int16 unmeasured on board.
int16_recognition.note_on_earlier_numbers: An earlier evaluation used stretch preprocessing and reported int8 8/20 vs int16 17/20, exaggerating both the absolute loss and the int16 advantage. Superseded by the pad numbers above.

wide_960_recognition.why: 48x320 truncates lines longer than 6.7:1; 960 handles up to ~20:1. Chosen for long-line workloads.

int16_960.board_result: On 21 long lines (float@960 reference), CER 7.5% (58/777). On genuinely long lines it is near-perfect: a 79-char Chinese sentence decoded exactly, 74/76-char lines with 1 error each, test.jpg (30 chars) with 1 insertion. The 7.5% is carried by short, hard crops, not the long lines 960 exists for.
int16_960.compile_note: hmct logged 2 onnxruntime Reshape errors on the Squeeze.3 node during calibration setup (a 5D attention reshape that onnxslim did not resolve for the 120-timestep shape). They did NOT affect the result: quant cosine 0.9981 and the board reproduces float@960 long-line decodes. Verdict that it is harmless comes from the BOARD, not the clean-compile -- exactly why compile success is never the gate.

int8_960.board_result: On the same 21 long lines: CER 17.5% (136/777), exact 2/21.
int8_960.note: int8 is notably worse than int16 on long lines (17.5% vs 7.5%). It is also LARGER: 30.7 MB vs int16's 27.7 MB, because the mixed int8/int16 graph fragments while all-int16 is one contiguous instruction stream.

wide_960_recognition.recommendation: For the 960 long-line build, use int16 (config_rec_int16_960.yaml). It halves the character error rate vs int8 AND produces a smaller .hbm. On genuinely long lines it is near-perfect; a 79-char sentence decoded exactly. Latency unmeasured on board.
wide_960_recognition.when_to_use_960: Only when lines routinely exceed 6.7:1. 960 is 3x the area of 320 and short lines waste it on padding. For mixed content, keep the 320 build and split long lines by detector box.
